topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like a physical weight pressing down on my chest. I stood by the entrance, watching the crowd mill about, a strange, cold knot tightening in my stomach. My canvases—my very soul poured onto linen—were hanging on the walls, vibrant and exposed under the harsh spotlights. They were mine. They had been my private world, my unfiltered thoughts made visible. Now, they were a spectacle, a commodity displayed without a single word of my consent. A deep flush crept up my neck, and I instinctively pulled my collar higher, wishing I could dissolve into the polished floor. Every glance felt like an accusation, and I wanted nothing more than to disappear into the shadows.

[story 2]

"They're beautiful, aren't they?" a voice drifted over. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that felt alien on my face. My hands, clammy beneath my jacket, clenched into fists inside my pockets. I watched a critic gesture toward a particularly abstract piece, and the sound of his approval echoed hollowly in the cavernous space. Internally, a churning nausea rose. It was a sharp, acidic feeling, like bile rising in my throat. I took a hesitant step backward, my heel catching on a stray piece of carpet. I needed to leave, to escape the suffocating gaze of the public, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer audacity of the situation.

[story 3]

I found myself lingering near the sculpture section, pretending intense interest in a nearby bronze. The air felt thick, heavy with judgment. Every time someone paused to look at my work, a distinct pressure built behind my eyes, a stinging behind the lids. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a burning sensation that had nothing to do with the gallery's climate control. A sudden, overwhelming urge to apologize, to snatch the pieces back, washed over me, immediately followed by a profound sense of helplessness. I lowered my gaze to my shoes, tracing the pattern on the sole with a trembling finger, trying to anchor myself against the unsettling reality of being utterly uninvited to my own creation.
